Thin-Film Interference
Objects spontaneously emit electromagnetic radiation from their thermal energy.
Internal energy comprises kinetic and potential energy; ideal gases lack potential energy.
Power depends on current and voltage across circuit elements.
Photon absorption or emission transfers energy in atom systems.
Atoms contain nuclei with protons and neutrons surrounded by electrons.
Quantum theory explains atomic phenomena; particles exhibit dual particle-wave behavior.
Energy needed to change temperature depends on material's specific heat capacity.
Series connections require all charges through each element; parallel connections allow multiple paths.
Light at medium interfaces is partially transmitted, reflected, and absorbed.
Double-slit patterns result from combined diffraction and interference effects.
Isolated system entropy never decreases; constant only for reversible processes.
Waves spread around obstacles and through openings via diffraction.
Charge is fundamental; electrons are −e, protons are +e, elementary charge indivisible.
Parallel-plate capacitor has two conducting plates holding opposite charges.
Wave interference occurs when two or more waves interact.
Doppler effect relates rest frequency, observed frequency, and relative velocity.
Charges moving through potential differences represent energy changes in circuits.
EM waves have perpendicular oscillating fields; transverse waves with planar fronts.
Waves reflect or transmit at boundaries; inversion depends on speed change.
Period, frequency, amplitude, wavelength characterize periodic waves; energy increases with frequency.
Waves transfer energy without matter; pulses are single disturbances; waves are periodic.
Parallel rays converge at focal point in convex lenses.
System charge changes via friction, contact, or induced polarization effects.
Junction rule stems from conservation of electric charge principle.
Light bends when traveling between different media.
Charged objects are sources of electric fields.
Equivalent capacitance combines multiple capacitors into single capacitor value.
Electric potential is electric potential energy measured per unit charge.
Light rays model geometric optics; wave nature needed for diffraction.
Magnetic flux measures perpendicular magnetic field component through area.
Electric potential energy equals work needed to assemble charged system.
Magnetic fields are vector fields from dipoles determining forces on charges.
Current-carrying wires produce magnetic fields with circular field lines; magnitude depends on current and distance.
Concave mirrors reflect parallel rays toward focal point.
Current measures charge flow rate; driven by potential difference in circuits.
Moving charge creates magnetic field depending on velocity, distance, and perpendicular direction.
Alpha particles, neutrinos, antineutrinos, and positrons have unique charge and mass properties.
Gas pressure results from atomic collisions analyzed via momentum conservation principles.
Charged object motion between different potentials changes electric potential energy.
Ideal gas model assumes random velocities, negligible atomic volumes, elastic collisions.
Circuits contain loops with various electrical elements like wires and batteries.
Strong force dominates nuclear-scale interactions between protons and neutrons.
Photon-electron collision reduces photon energy and increases wavelength.
Thermal contact enables energy transfer; heating and cooling are directional thermal processes.
Resistance quantifies how much an object opposes electric charge movement.
Photoelectric effect: electromagnetic radiation causes electron emission from materials.
Electromagnetic waves propagate through vacuum without requiring a medium.
Temperature characterizes average atomic kinetic energy; Maxwell-Boltzmann distribution relates speeds to temperature.
Ideal gas equation relates pressure, volume, moles, temperature via PV equals nRT.
Three mechanisms transfer thermal energy: conduction, convection, and radiation.
Internal energy changes alter system structure without affecting center of mass motion.
Specific heat is intrinsic property determined by atomic structure.
Entropy describes energy dispersal and unavailable work; state function; maximum at equilibrium.
Point charge model treats charged objects as negligibly small in analysis.
Net charge changes only through electron transfer between system and surroundings.
Electric field is force per unit charge; points away from positive and toward negative charges.
Electric potential energy follows a general mathematical equation form.
Electric potential from multiple charges equals sum of individual potentials.
Capacitance depends on plate area, separation, and dielectric material properties.
Potential difference causes kinetic energy change; total energy conserved.
Current direction follows positive charge motion; electrons actually move opposite.
Closed loops allow charge flow; open circuits prevent it; short circuits bypass voltage.
Resistance depends on resistivity, length, and cross-sectional area; resistivity increases with temperature.
Bulb brightness increases with power dissipation in the bulb.
Series resistances add; parallel resistance inverses add; parallel lowers total resistance.
Kirchhoff's loop rule follows from energy conservation principle.
Charge entering junction per unit time equals charge exiting.
Series capacitors maintain equal charge magnitude on plates.
Magnetic fields are vectors shown by closed-loop field lines from north to south.
Flux depends on perpendicular field and area; sign indicates field direction.
Magnetic forces govern interactions between moving charged objects.
Magnetic fields exert forces on current-carrying wires; force depends on current, length, field strength, and angle.
Ray diagrams show light path through matter interactions.
Convex mirrors reflect parallel rays from virtual focal point.
Light speed changes cause refraction in new media.
Parallel rays diverge from focal point in concave lenses.
Mechanical waves need a medium; electromagnetic waves do not.
Sinusoidal waves described by displacement equations as functions of time or position.
Transverse waves can be polarized to single plane; longitudinal waves cannot.
Greater relative velocity increases frequency difference; relative motion determines frequency shift direction.
Interacting waves pass through each other and overlap without bouncing.
Diffraction is strongest when opening size matches wavelength.
Double-slit interference patterns demonstrate light's wave nature via Young's experiment.
Reflected ray phase shift depends on refractive index comparison at interface.
Light exhibits wave-particle duality; photon energy depends on frequency.
Proton count defines elements; electron arrangement and isotopes affect interactions.
Energy absorption or emission requires matching energy differences between atomic states.
Blackbodies absorb all incident radiation and emit energy at thermal equilibrium.
Threshold frequency determines electron emission; photon count irrelevant.
Compton effect demonstrates photons as particles; energy-momentum conservation explains photon property changes.
Nuclear reactions must conserve the total number of nucleons.
Four decay types conserve nucleon number, lepton number, and charge in nuclear processes.
Superposition: total displacement equals sum of individual displacements.
Nuclear reactions conserve energy, mass-energy equivalence, and momentum.
Electrostatic force is proportional to charges, inversely proportional to distance squared.
Wave speed depends on wave type and medium properties; constants for light; formula for strings.
Blackbody spectrum depends only on temperature; described by Planck's law and Wien's displacement law.
Specific isotopes determine which radioactive decay type occurs.
EM spectrum ordered by wavelength: radio to gamma rays; visible light subdivided by color.
Wave polarization can reduce intensity, measured as power per unit area.
Photon wavelength shift depends on scattering angle change.
Wavelength proportional to speed, inversely proportional to frequency.
Graphs of pressure, temperature, volume describe and determine gas properties.
Potential difference is change in potential energy per unit charge moved.
Circuit elements can participate in more than one electrical loop.
Electron energy independent of photon count; supports photon quantization.
Faraday's law relates changing flux to induced emf.
Incident light reflects from surfaces.
Magnetic force on moving charge depends on charge, velocity, field, angle.
Sum of potential differences around a closed loop equals zero.
Ideal batteries and wires have negligible resistance; emf measured at terminals with no current.
Electric field is a vector; net field is superposition; visualized via field maps and lines.
Electric field between plates is constant; charged particles undergo constant acceleration.
Plane mirrors have focal point at infinite distance.
Magnetic dipoles arise from charge motion; poles attract/repel; field decreases with distance.
Ohm's law relates current, resistance, and voltage; ohmic materials have constant resistance and resistivity.
Energy transitions produce photons of single frequency and wavelength.
First law conserves energy through work, heat, and internal energy changes.
Bohr model explains discrete energy states using classical circular orbits and standing waves.
Photon speed depends on medium; constant in vacuum, reduced in materials.
Grounding connects charged system to large neutral system like Earth.
Refracted waves do not experience phase change crossing medium boundaries.
Refractive index inversely relates to light speed.
Time constant τ = RC characterizes capacitor charge and discharge rates.
Conduction rate depends on thermal conductivity, geometry, temperature difference.
Double-slit pattern visualizations reveal physical properties of slits and waves.
Total system energy equals sum of all pairwise interaction energies.
Real images form when refracted rays intersect at common point.
Spontaneous thermal energy flows from hot to cold via atomic collisions until equilibrium.
Multiple diffracted wavefronts create observable interference patterns.
Entropy change determined by surroundings interaction; isolated systems equilibrate; closed systems entropy can decrease.
Constructive and destructive interference produce varying amplitudes in resultant waves.
Moving charge experiences independent forces from electric and magnetic fields.
Maximum kinetic energy depends on light frequency and material work function.
Lenz's law determines induced emf direction opposing flux change.
Conductor charges reside on surface; interior field is zero; surface field is perpendicular.
Incident angle equals reflected angle measured from surface normal.
Spherical mirror focal point lies halfway to center of curvature.
Snell's law relates angles and indices; rays bend away or toward normal.
Absolute zero temperature extrapolated from pressure versus temperature graph.
Virtual images form when diverging rays appear to originate from point.
Transverse waves have disturbance perpendicular to propagation direction.
Like charges repel, opposite charges attract; force is along separation line.
Mass and energy interchange in nuclear reactions via E = mc².
Single-slit diffraction creates bright and dark bands through interference.
Thermal conductivity is intrinsic property determined by atomic arrangement.
Diffraction gratings combine many slit patterns to produce intense interference patterns.
Thin films produce interference when thickness is comparable to wavelength.
Particles exhibit wave properties; de Broglie wavelength inversely relates to momentum.
Work on system equals negative pressure times volume change.
Schematics represent circuits; arrangement affects properties; symbols show variable elements.
Stored electric potential energy equals work done separating charges.
Each element has unique spectral frequencies identifying its composition.
Nonideal battery internal resistance modeled as series resistor with ideal battery.
Thermal equilibrium occurs when no net energy transfers between systems in contact.
Electric potential variation in a loop can be graphed versus position.
Voltage and current in RC circuits change exponentially toward steady state.
Touching conductors redistribute charge until surfaces reach equal potential.
Magnetic dipoles align with external magnetic fields like compass needles.
Released nuclear energy appears as kinetic energy of products or photons.
Material composition determines magnetic response: ferromagnetic, paramagnetic, or diamagnetic.
Hall effect describes potential difference from perpendicular magnetic field.
Electric potential energy in capacitor expressed mathematically.
Thin lens equation relates image location, object distance, focal length.
Real images form where reflected rays actually intersect.
Average electric field equals potential difference divided by distance.
Diffraction gratings disperse white light into colors by wavelength in spectrum.
Total internal reflection occurs beyond critical angle threshold.
Opening shape determines the resulting diffraction pattern.
Rough surfaces scatter light in many directions via diffuse reflection.
Visual diagrams help determine results of wave interactions.
Practical applications include soap bubbles, oil films, and antireflection coatings.
PV diagrams show processes; area under curve represents work magnitude.
Longitudinal waves have disturbance parallel to propagation; sound is mechanical longitudinal.
Work function: minimum energy needed to eject electrons from material.
Rod on rails exemplifies electromagnetic induction in uniform field.
Bound quantum systems have discrete quantized energy and momentum values.
Insulator charges distribute throughout interior and surface; interior field can be nonzero.
Contact forces are convenient macroscopic models, though electric forces underlie them.
Current in nonideal battery reduces terminal voltage due to internal resistance.
Binding energy ionizes atoms; ground state requires maximum energy removal.
Amplitude is maximum displacement; determines loudness and energy in waves.
Diffraction patterns reveal slit and wave physical properties.
Earth's magnetic field approximates a dipole field configuration.
Fusion combines smaller nuclei into larger nuclei and subatomic particles.
Dielectric increases capacitance and creates opposing internal electric field.
Virtual images form where reflected rays appear to originate.
K_max equation relates photon energy, frequency, and work function.
Smooth surfaces uniformly reflect light via specular reflection.
Field maps and equipotential lines describe charge configurations and predict motion.
Electrostatic forces are attractive or repulsive; gravity only attracts.
Special thermal processes include isovolumetric, isothermal, isobaric, and adiabatic.
Beats from slightly different frequencies show periodic amplitude changes.
Magnification equals image height ratio and image distance ratio.
Fission splits nuclei into smaller nuclei and subatomic particles.
Image location depends on focal length and object distance via mirror equation.
Magnetic permeability measures material magnetization response to external fields.
Ray diagrams using three principal rays determine image properties.
Electrostatic force magnitude typically exceeds gravitational force magnitude.
Experimental apparatus uses illuminated plates and adjustable voltage to determine work function.
Standing waves have nodes of zero amplitude and antinodes of maximum amplitude.
Magnification is image-to-object size ratio, depending on positions.
Gravity dominates at large scales because large systems are electrically neutral.
Fission occurs spontaneously or requires energy input based on binding energy.
Vacuum permeability µ0 is constant for free space in physics equations.
Radioactive decay spontaneously transforms nuclei; individual timing indeterminable but probabilistic. Half-life is decay time for half nuclei; λ = ln2/t₁/₂.
Material permeability varies with composition and arrangement, differs from vacuum.
Electric permittivity measures how much a material polarizes in electric field.
Ray diagrams using three principal rays determine image properties.
Polarization is electron rearrangement induced by external electric field.
Decay constant predicts remaining nuclei or determines material age.
Half-lives vary enormously across unstable elements and isotopes.
